A surname of one of the main characters, Seiji Amasawa.
A category of Japanese comics and animated films aimed at a young female audience, often focusing on romance, relationships, and personal growth, which aligns with the film's themes.
A card issued by a library that allows a patron to borrow books. The film prominently features characters checking out books using these cards.
A person who makes or repairs stringed instruments. Seiji aspires to become one, which is a significant plot point.
The act of composing a fictional story. Shizuku decides to pursue this as a way to find her own passion.
An adjective describing qualities characteristic of Studio Ghibli films, such as beautiful animation, heartfelt stories, and often a touch of magic or wonder, even in realistic settings.
A phrase used to describe the process of growing older with dignity and poise. While not explicitly stated, the film subtly touches upon this theme through its characters' journeys.

background.summary: Whisper of the Heart is a beloved animated film that follows the burgeoning romance and personal growth of Shizuku Tsukishima, a young girl who loves to read, and Seiji Amasawa, a fellow student with a passion for craftsmanship. As Shizuku navigates her adolescent dreams and uncertainties, she finds inspiration in Seiji's clear aspirations, leading her to discover her own path.
context: The film is based on a manga of the same name by Aoi Hiiragi.
It is the only feature film directed by Yoshifumi Kondô.
Studio Ghibli produced the film, with Hayao Miyazaki contributing to the screenplay and production.
The story is set in a realistic, contemporary Tokyo, a departure from some of Ghibli's more fantastical settings.
background.director_note: Yoshifumi Kondô was a highly respected animator at Studio Ghibli, known for his meticulous attention to detail and his grounded, realistic portrayal of everyday life and human emotions. 'Whisper of the Heart' was his sole directorial feature. Before this, he worked as an animation director on acclaimed films like 'Grave of the Fireflies' and 'Kiki's Delivery Service,' and as a character designer for 'Only Yesterday.' His directorial work in 'Whisper of the Heart' is characterized by its gentle pacing, subtle character development, and a keen eye for the nuances of adolescence and the beauty found in ordinary moments. Kondô's untimely death in 1998 meant his potential as a director was tragically cut short, leaving 'Whisper of the Heart' as his lasting legacy.
background.wikipedia: Whisper of the Heart is a 1995 Japanese animated coming-of-age drama film directed by Yoshifumi Kondō and written by Hayao Miyazaki based on Aoi Hiiragi's 1989 manga Mimi o Sumaseba. Produced by Studio Ghibli and distributed by Toho, the film stars Yoko Honna, Issey Takahashi, Takashi Tachibana, Shigeru Muroi, Shigeru Tsuyuguchi and Keiju Kobayashi.

sections.title: The Ordinary Days
sections.content: Shizuku Tsukishima is a junior high school student who loves reading and often borrows books from the library. She notices that many of the books she chooses have been previously checked out by the same person: Seiji Amasawa.

sections.title: A Chance Encounter
sections.content: Curiosity piqued, Shizuku tries to find out who Seiji is. She follows a mysterious cat, 'The Baron,' and eventually encounters Seiji, who is an aspiring cellist. They develop a friendly rivalry and bond over their shared passion for books and dreams.

sections.title: Diverging Paths
sections.content: Shizuku learns that Seiji's family owns an antique shop and that he plans to study in Cremona, Italy, to become a luthier. This news deeply affects Shizuku, who feels her own life lacks direction and purpose compared to Seiji's clear ambition.

sections.title: The Weight of Dreams
sections.content: Feeling inadequate, Shizuku abandons her studies and attempts to write her own novel, hoping to find her own path. This period is fraught with self-doubt and frustration, symbolized by her struggle to complete her story. The Baron, a ceramic cat in her grandfather's shop, serves as a silent confidant.

sections.title: Whispers of the Future
sections.content: Shizuku eventually completes her novel and presents it to Seiji upon his return from Italy. He reads it and offers his honest feedback, acknowledging her talent. They share a hopeful moment, agreeing to pursue their dreams and perhaps reunite in the future, with Shizuku now more confident in her own aspirations.

plot_summary.theme: The film explores the uncertainties and challenges of adolescence, self-discovery, and the pursuit of dreams, highlighting the importance of passion and perseverance.

characters.name: Shizuku Tsukishima
characters.description: A curious and imaginative middle schooler who loves reading, she embarks on a journey of self-discovery as she grapples with her future aspirations and the fear of not being good enough.

characters.name: Seiji Amasawa
characters.description: A diligent and ambitious young boy with a passion for music and craftsmanship, he inspires Shizuku and serves as a foil to her initial uncertainty about her future.

characters.name: The Baron
characters.description: A sophisticated, anthropomorphic cat figurine from an antique shop, he represents wisdom, encouragement, and the romantic ideal, acting as a muse for Shizuku's creative endeavors.

characters.name: Shiro Nishi
characters.description: The kindly owner of the antique shop 'Gekko' and grandfather to Seiji, he offers gentle guidance and perspective to Shizuku.

easter_eggs.detail: The mysterious cat, The Baron, is a character from Aoi Hiiragi's manga 'Baron: The Cat Returns,' which later became a Studio Ghibli film.
The antique shop, 'Gekko,' is filled with various trinkets and objects that hint at the characters' deeper interests and the film's themes.
Shizuku's journey to write a novel mirrors the creative process and the struggles many artists face in finding their voice.
The recurring sight of the mysterious cat and its eventual connection to The Baron hints at a magical realism element.

spoiler_fun_facts.fact: The film is based on a manga of the same name by Aoi Hiiragi, which was serialized in 'Ribbon' magazine. The anime expands significantly on the original story, particularly in developing Seiji's character and Shizuku's internal struggles.

Director Hayao Miyazaki wrote the screenplay, though he gave director duties to Yoshifumi Kondō, whom he intended to be his successor. Kondō's tragic early death cut short his promising directorial career.
The scenes where Shizuku struggles with writing her novel and contemplates her future were deeply personal for Kondō, who was also grappling with his own creative path at the time.
The detailed animation of the antique shop, the cityscape, and the characters' movements reflects the animation team's commitment to realism and capturing the nuances of everyday life.

fun_facts.fact: The film was inspired by the manga of the same name by Aoi Hiiragi, which was created to be a "visual poem".
Hayao Miyazaki wrote the screenplay over a period of about a year, driven by his own childhood experiences and memories.
The iconic 'The Baron' character was originally created for another Studio Ghibli film that never materialized.
Director Yoshifumi Kondō's only feature film, his untimely death shortly after its release deeply impacted Studio Ghibli.
The flying sequences and cat motifs were heavily influenced by Hayao Miyazaki's personal interests and recurring themes.
The film's setting in Tokyo's Tama New Town was chosen for its realistic depiction of suburban life.

tt0113824_facts_en.first_act_hint: The first act gently introduces Shizuku Tsukishima, a young, imaginative girl navigating the cusp of adolescence and the joy of reading. Expect a slice-of-life feel, observing her daily routines and burgeoning curiosity about the world around her, especially as she encounters a mysterious library patron.

tt0113824_verdict_en.english_note: Dialogue is simple, clear, and spoken at a moderate pace.

tt0137523_meta.title: Fight Club

background.summary: 《搏击俱乐部》（Fight Club）是一部由大卫·芬奇执导，改编自恰克·帕拉尼克的同名小说的犯罪、剧情、惊悚电影。故事讲述了一位深受失眠困扰的办公室职员与一位玩世不恭的肥皂推销员偶然相识，并共同创立了一个地下搏击俱乐部，这个俱乐部最终演变成了一个远超他们最初预期的地下运动。影片以其非线性的叙事、尖锐的社会批判和令人震惊的结局而闻名，深刻探讨了消费主义、男性身份危机以及现代社会中的异化等主题。
context: 电影《搏击俱乐部》因其大胆的叙事和深刻的社会评论而成为一部具有里程碑意义的作品。
影片对消费主义文化进行了辛辣的讽刺，揭示了物质主义如何导致人们的迷失和空虚。
故事的核心围绕着主人公的身份认同危机以及在现代社会中寻求存在意义的挣扎展开。
影片的结局是其最令人津津乐道的部分之一，颠覆了观众的预期，引发了广泛的讨论。
background.director_note: 大卫·芬奇（David Fincher）以其对黑暗、犯罪和心理惊悚题材的精湛驾驭而闻名，并以其标志性的冷峻、精准的视觉风格和对细节的极致追求著称。他的电影常常探讨人性的阴暗面、社会的腐败以及角色的内心挣扎。代表作包括《七宗罪》（Se7en）、《搏击俱乐部》（Fight Club）、《社交网络》（The Social Network）、《消失的爱人》（Gone Girl）等。芬奇尤其擅长使用阴郁的色调、冷冽的光影和有节奏感的剪辑来营造压抑、紧张的氛围，并通过精心设计的构图和对细节的关注，引导观众深入角色的心理世界。他在《搏击俱乐部》中运用了快速的剪辑、画外音以及非传统的叙事手法，极大地增强了影片的冲击力和观赏性。
background.wikipedia: Fight Club is a 1999 American film directed by David Fincher and starring Brad Pitt, Edward Norton, and Helena Bonham Carter. It is based on the 1996 novel by Chuck Palahniuk. Norton plays the unnamed narrator, who is discontented with his white-collar job. He forms a "fight club" with a soap salesman, Tyler Durden (Pitt), and becomes embroiled with an impoverished but beguiling woman, Marla Singer.

sections.title: 窒息的生活
sections.content: 叙事者是一个患有失眠症的普通白领，他对机械、重复的消费主义生活感到极度不满和麻木。为了寻求真实的感觉，他开始参加各种互助会，试图从中找到慰藉，但收效甚微。

sections.title: 泰勒的出现
sections.content: 在一次飞机上，叙事者遇到了极具魅力的旅行肥皂推销员泰勒·德顿。两人一拍即合，回到住处后，在一次醉酒后，泰勒提议互相殴打，这意外的刺激感让叙事者摆脱了长期的失眠。随后，他们在家中创建了一个地下拳击俱乐部，最初只是为了给像他们一样感到生活空虚的男人们一个发泄的场所。

sections.title: 失控的计划
sections.content: 地下拳击俱乐部迅速发展壮大，吸引了越来越多的人，并逐渐演变成一个名为“散打”（Project Mayhem）的地下组织。该组织以反消费主义、反现代文明为宗旨，开始进行一系列破坏性的“艺术”行动，包括涂鸦、纵火和破坏公物，泰勒成为了这股狂热思潮的绝对领导者。

sections.title: 身份的幻灭
sections.content: 随着事件的升级，叙事者发现自己对泰勒越来越失控的计划感到恐惧和不安，他试图阻止泰勒，却发现自己和泰勒在行为和思想上越来越界限模糊。更令人震惊的是，他发现所谓的“泰勒”根本不是一个独立的人，而是他自己分裂出来的另一人格，所有疯狂的行为都是他潜意识的投射。

sections.title: 自我毁灭与新生
sections.content: 在最终的摊牌中，叙事者（作为真正的“我”）在“泰勒”人格的威胁下，终于下定决心，用枪射击了自己的口腔，以此来“杀死”他，摆脱这个失控的自我。最终，在意识模糊之际，他挽着玛拉的手，看着自己一手策划的“散打”组织正在执行的炸毁摩天大楼的计划，他选择与过去一同毁灭，也可能迎来某种新生。

plot_summary.theme: 在物欲横流的现代社会，个体通过极端反叛来寻找存在的意义与真实，但最终可能走向自我毁灭。

characters.name: Narrator
characters.description: 一个被消费主义和失眠困扰的都市白领，渴望摆脱空虚，在泰勒的影响下走向极端反叛，最终发现并试图消灭自己分裂出的人格。

characters.name: Tyler Durden
characters.description: 极具魅力、反叛不羁的肥皂推销员，是叙事者潜意识分裂出的人格，代表着打破一切束缚的原始欲望和破坏力。

characters.name: Marla Singer
characters.description: 与叙事者一样在互助会寻求慰藉的神秘女子，她与叙事者和泰勒之间形成了复杂而扭曲的情感纠葛。

easter_eggs.detail: 电影中多次闪现泰勒·德顿的影像，尤其是在叙事者看到电脑屏幕上闪烁的广告背景时，是暗示其分裂人格的重要线索。
叙事者第一次被泰勒“殴打”的场景，背景中有一个短暂的、几乎看不见的泰勒·德顿闪现，预示着他是叙事者分裂出来的人格。
电影中反复出现的消费品牌标志，如宜家家具，强化了影片对消费主义的批判主题。
影片结尾，叙事者与玛拉手牵手看着大楼倒塌，这或许是致敬《搏击俱乐部》原著小说的结局，也暗示了叙事者接受了彻底的毁灭。

spoiler_fun_facts.fact: 布拉德·皮特在拍摄期间为了更贴近泰勒·德顿的形象，特意学习并练习了肥皂制作，还自己制作了电影中出现的肥皂。
导演大卫·芬奇故意在影片的许多场景中，在画面中快速闪过泰勒·德顿的影像（每隔几分钟一次），这是暗示叙事者精神分裂的关键细节，只有留心观察的观众才能发现。
在影片高潮，叙事者开枪“杀死”泰勒人格时，他选择的是朝自己的嘴里开枪，这个极端的自残行为象征着他试图彻底根除这个失控的“自我”。
电影中的“散打”（Project Mayhem）这个组织，其行动模式和理念很大程度上参考了左翼无政府主义团体的行为，以及一些社会运动的激进手段。

fun_facts.fact: 电影中的所有香皂都是由剧组人员手工制作的，其中一些香皂的制作过程甚至出现在了正片中。
布拉德·皮特和爱德华·诺顿为了更好地扮演角色，接受了拳击和陶艺训练。皮特还特意去看了UFC比赛。
为了表现主角失眠的状态，导演大卫·芬奇在影片开头故意加入了模糊的、快速闪过的画面。
原著小说作者恰克·帕拉尼克在电影拍摄期间曾到访片场，他对电影的改编表示满意，认为它抓住了小说的精神。
影片中出现的“无政府主义”和“消费主义”等概念，在当时引发了广泛的社会讨论，使得这部电影在上映后多年仍被奉为经典。
为了拍摄地下拳击俱乐部场景，剧组特意选择了废弃的屠宰场，增加了现场的原始和粗粝感。

tt0137523_facts.first_act_hint: 影片开篇将带你进入一个充满压抑和疏离感的都市生活。你会看到一位饱受失眠困扰的普通白领，在看似规律却极其乏味的生活中寻找出口，直到一次意外的邂逅，开启了他人生中前所未有的改变。

tt0137523_verdict.one_line_verdict: 一部颠覆性的心理惊悚片，适合寻求震撼体验和深刻思考的观众，影院观看能最大化其视听冲击力与沉浸感。
good_for: 喜欢大卫·芬奇风格的影迷
对社会议题感兴趣的思考者
追求强烈视听体验的观众
not_good_for: 对暴力血腥镜头敏感的观众
tt0137523_verdict.english_note: 语速稍快，包含一些俚语和生活化表达，理解需一定基础。
tt0137523_verdict.one_line_summary: 颠覆三观的经典之作，暗黑美学与社会批判的完美结合。

vocabulary.explanation: A person who suffers from insomnia, a sleep disorder characterized by difficulty falling or staying asleep.
Someone who produces soap, often as a craft or small business.
Operating in secrecy, often outside the law or public view.
Carefree, reckless, and unconcerned about consequences.
A social and economic order that encourages the acquisition of goods and services.
Opposed to or critical of consumerism.
A secret organization where men engage in fistfights.
A narrator whose credibility is compromised, often due to mental instability, bias, or deceit.
A mental disorder characterized by the presence of two or more distinct personality states. (Spoiler hint: key to the plot)
A key character who becomes entangled with the narrator.
The charismatic and anarchic soap salesman who co-founds Fight Club.
The escalation of Fight Club into a more organized, destructive, and anarchic movement.

background.summary: A man suffering from insomnia and a dissolute life meets a charismatic soap salesman, and together they create a secret fight club. This club escalates into a violent, anti-consumerist movement that blurs the lines between reality and delusion.
context: The film is based on the 1996 novel of the same name by Chuck Palahniuk.
It is known for its dark, satirical commentary on consumerism, masculinity, and modern society.
The narrative structure is non-linear and features an unreliable narrator.
background.director_note: David Fincher is renowned for his distinctive visual style, often characterized by a dark, gritty, and meticulously crafted aesthetic. He frequently employs a desaturated color palette and a precise, often unsettling, camera work. Notable films include 'Se7en' (1995), 'The Social Network' (2010), and 'Zodiac' (2007), which showcase his mastery of suspense, pacing, and character psychology. Fincher's filmmaking techniques often involve extensive pre-visualization, meticulous set design, and a keen eye for detail, creating immersive and psychologically intense cinematic experiences.
background.wikipedia: Fight Club is a 1999 American film directed by David Fincher and starring Brad Pitt, Edward Norton, and Helena Bonham Carter. It is based on the 1996 novel by Chuck Palahniuk. Norton plays the unnamed narrator, who is discontented with his white-collar job. He forms a "fight club" with a soap salesman, Tyler Durden (Pitt), and becomes embroiled with an impoverished but beguiling woman, Marla Singer.

sections.title: The Narrator's Unraveling
sections.content: A deeply dissatisfied and insomniac office worker, plagued by consumerism and a lack of purpose, seeks solace through support groups, finding a bizarre connection with others who share his pain. His life takes a radical turn when he meets Tyler Durden, a charismatic and anarchic soap salesman, after a plane crash. Their initial, clandestine boxing matches in a bar parking lot soon escalate into a nationwide phenomenon: Fight Club.

sections.title: Fight Club's Ascendance
sections.content: Fight Club grows exponentially, attracting a cult-like following and expanding to multiple cities, operating under a strict set of rules. The Narrator finds himself increasingly estranged from his former life and his possessive girlfriend, Marla Singer, while becoming more entwined with Tyler's increasingly dangerous philosophy and escalating acts of vandalism. The 'Project Mayhem' initiative begins, turning the members into a paramilitary force carrying out increasingly destructive missions.

sections.title: The Devastating Revelation
sections.content: The Narrator, horrified by the escalating chaos and destruction caused by Project Mayhem, attempts to stop Tyler, only to discover the terrifying truth: Tyler Durden is not a separate person but his own alter ego, a split personality created by his repressed desires and disillusionment. He realizes he has been orchestrating all of Project Mayhem's actions himself, including the murder of his boss and the elaborate bombing plan.

sections.title: A Pyrrhic Victory
sections.content: In the film's climax, the Narrator confronts his Tyler Durden persona in a New York skyscraper, the target of Project Mayhem's final act of destruction. A brutal fight ensues, and the Narrator, realizing he must kill himself to kill Tyler, shoots himself. As he falls, he holds hands with Marla, watching the buildings explode, his act of self-destruction simultaneously destroying his alter ego and his meticulously crafted destructive plan.

plot_summary.theme: The film explores themes of anti-consumerism, toxic masculinity, identity crisis, and the search for meaning in a meaningless modern world, ultimately portraying the destructive consequences of repressed desires.

characters.name: Narrator
characters.description: An insomniac office worker consumed by consumerism, he seeks meaning through support groups and eventually creates the violent alter ego, Tyler Durden, to break free from his mundane existence.

characters.name: Tyler Durden
characters.description: The charismatic and anarchic soap salesman who embodies the Narrator's repressed desires for freedom and rebellion, leading him and others into destructive nihilism.

characters.name: Marla Singer
characters.description: A fellow support group 'hustler' who becomes entangled with the Narrator and Tyler, representing a dark, nihilistic connection and a potential path to redemption.

characters.name: Robert 'Bob' Paulson
characters.description: A physically imposing man whose support group experience and subsequent actions highlight the film's themes of masculine identity and the search for belonging.

easter_eggs.detail: Brief flashes of Tyler Durden appear before his official introduction, foreshadowing his connection to the Narrator.
The movie is filled with subliminal product placement (e.g., Starbucks cups) reinforcing the theme of consumerism, often appearing just before or after moments of violence or destruction.
The recurring imagery of soap, particularly the pristine white bar Tyler uses, symbolizes purification and erasure, reflecting the characters' desire to wash away their old lives.
The single yellow stripe on the Narrator's tie in his first scene is a subtle nod to Tyler Durden's yellow shirt, hinting at their connection.

spoiler_fun_facts.fact: The 'I am Jack's...' monologues, which the Narrator uses to process his experiences, are a direct homage to the 'I am Joe's...' series of autobiographical essays popularized in the magazine 'Reader's Digest'.
Edward Norton trained rigorously in boxing for the role, while Brad Pitt only had basic training as the filmmakers wanted Tyler to appear more naturally aggressive and improvisational.
The iconic soap-making scene was filmed using actual lye, which is highly caustic, posing a real danger to the actors. Helena Bonham Carter accidentally swallowed some during filming.

Director David Fincher intentionally made the ending ambiguous, with the final gunshot potentially being interpreted as the Narrator succumbing to Tyler or finally overcoming him, leaving the audience to decide.

fun_facts.fact: Edward Norton's character's apartment was intentionally designed to be minimalist, only to be destroyed and rebuilt multiple times during filming as a visual metaphor.
The iconic soap bars used in the film were actually made from real soap by a professional soap maker, not props.
To prepare for his role, Brad Pitt actually learned how to make soap from scratch.
The "Fight Club" rules were printed on the back of movie tickets in some releases, adding an interactive element for audiences.
Director David Fincher initially wanted to shoot the film in Los Angeles but moved production to his hometown of Minneapolis to capture a specific aesthetic.
The "single frame" subliminal flashes of Tyler Durden were inserted into the film before any dialogue scenes featuring him, a subtle visual cue.

tt0137523_facts_en.first_act_hint: The film opens with a sense of profound existential ennui and detachment. You'll be introduced to a protagonist struggling with insomnia and a monotonous corporate life, searching for meaning in consumerism.

tt0137523_verdict_en.one_line_verdict: A mind-bending cult classic that demands a theatrical rewatch for its shocking twists and intense atmosphere, perfect for those who love psychological thrillers.
tt0137523_verdict_en.english_note: Fast-paced dialogue, some slang, and philosophical concepts may require close attention.
tt0137523_verdict_en.one_line_summary: An insomniac's descent into anarchy via a secret society of bare-knuckle brawlers.
